title: stdio vs SSE/HTTP transports trade off coupling and reach
description: stdio binds a server to one local host process lifecycle; SSE/HTTP transports allow remote, multi-client servers but add auth, network, and connection-lifetime concerns.
body: Use when choosing or debugging how a host talks to an MCP server. stdio runs the server as a child process communicating over standard streams: simple, locally sandboxed, and tied to the parent's lifetime, but single-client and not remotable. SSE/HTTP transports let a server run as a network service for multiple/remote clients but require authentication, TLS, connection keep-alive, and reconnect handling. Do not expose an stdio-only tool over the network without an auth boundary. Actions: pick stdio for local single-host integrations and SSE/HTTP only with auth and transport security. Validation signals include a transport-specific connection test and, for SSE/HTTP, an auth-required check. Risk boundary: a network-reachable MCP server with no authentication is an exposure; flag missing auth before deploying remotely.
